summary:
The Battle of Mohacs (August 29, 1526 AD) was a catastrophic Hungarian defeat at which [[event:2401|Suleiman the Magnificent's]] Ottoman army annihilated the Hungarian royal army in under two hours, killing King Louis II of Hungary and Bohemia, and effectively ending the independent Kingdom of Hungary — one of the most consequential battles in Central European history. The Hungarian force of approximately 25,000 was outmatched in numbers (Ottoman forces estimated at 50,000–100,000), technology (Ottoman artillery), and tactical coordination; Louis II chose to fight in the open plain near the Danube, giving the Ottomans a battlefield ideal for their combination of artillery, Janissary infantry, and cavalry.

The battle's outcome was almost total: the Hungarian army was destroyed in under two hours of fighting, Louis II drowned fleeing the battlefield, and approximately 14,000–20,000 Hungarians were killed, including most of the kingdom's nobility and clergy. Suleiman marched on to Buda (the Hungarian capital) and sacked it before withdrawing to consolidate. The battle set off a crisis in Central European politics: the Habsburg Archduke Ferdinand I claimed Hungary and Bohemia through his wife (Louis II's sister), while the Transylvanian prince John Zápolya claimed Hungary as an Ottoman vassal. The resulting tripartite division of Hungary — Ottoman Hungary, Habsburg Royal Hungary, and the semi-autonomous Transylvania — persisted for over 150 years and defined the [[event:2324|Ottoman-Habsburg]] frontier conflict. Mohacs remained a defining trauma in Hungarian national memory: the anniversary is commemorated as a day of national mourning, and the phrase 'more was lost at Mohacs' became a common Hungarian expression for catastrophic misfortune.

read_more_links.title: Louis II Hungary death drowning after Mohacs
